sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Breathing pattern disorder can drive breathlessness, chest tightness, dizziness, and activity intolerance, but it is a diagnosis of inclusion only after serious cardiopulmonary causes have been considered appropriately.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Disproportionate breathlessness
- Upper chest breathing, frequent sighing, air hunger, or inability to take a satisfying breath
- Dizziness, tingling, chest tightness, or throat tightness
- Symptoms worsened by stress, exertion, conversation, or enclosed spaces

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Do not label the patient with dysfunctional breathing until asthma, PE, ACS, pneumothorax, severe anaemia, and other important pathology are considered
- Many patients have overlapping anxiety, asthma, pain, or post-viral syndromes
- Pulse oximetry can be normal even when the patient feels profoundly breathless

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen acute respiratory distress, chest pain, hypoxia, and red flags before giving breathing-pattern advice
- Encourage calm pacing, posture, and slowing of over-breathing if the patient is otherwise stable
- Escalate if symptoms are new, severe, exertional, or medically unexplained

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Assess breathing mechanics, trigger situations, activity tolerance, and overlap with neck, rib, or anxiety drivers
- Use simple breathing retraining, pacing, and exposure strategies when appropriate
- Coordinate medical review or respiratory referral when symptoms are persistent, recurrent, or diagnostically unclear

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Breathing retraining should feel practical and calming, not like another performance task for the patient to fail
